old_claim.note: Early Copyright & Printing Privileges is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the scoped conservation practices are grounded in painting materials, workshop techniques, examination, cleaning, repair, repainting, and reframing, not in legal privileges for printing and publication.

source_shape.source_locator: Getty's Issues in the Conservation of Paintings page frames the field around fifteenth-century-to-present conservation readings, history of the profession, artists' materials and techniques, structural interventions, and cleaning/restoration.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source-backed scope for the node is painting conservation history and treatment practice, especially materials, techniques, structural interventions, and cleaning or restoration.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports removing a legal-printing privilege edge because copyright and printing licenses do not supply the material, craft, or treatment mechanism described for art conservation workshops.

ontology_before: Art Conservation Workshops depended on Early Copyright & Printing Privileges, implying legal print monopolies were a historical foundation for restoration and conservation practice.
ontology_after: Art Conservation Workshops are routed through painting and pigment-material practice, keeping the node scoped to object treatment and material examination rather than publication law.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: art_conservation_workshops no longer directly depends on early_copyright_printing_privileges and now preserves a direct materials-practice dependency.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to copyright administration for art manuals, prints, or publications rather than object conservation and restoration.
A stronger source shows early copyright or printing privileges were a direct prerequisite for the first conservation workshops rather than only a distant legal context.
short_reason: Painting conservation practice is source-backed through materials and treatment traditions, not through printing-privilege law.
